description:
Your role: you are a DevSecOps engineer who must benchmark a
  micro-operation and record the result as “policy-as-code”.
  Carry out a very small performance test (≈3–4 shell commands) on the
  local machine and decide whether it complies with the policy that the
  operation must finish in under **1000 ms**.  
  When you finish, write a compliance log that automated tests can read.

  1. Benchmark target  
     • Use the absolute command **/bin/true** (it does nothing and exits
       immediately).  
     • Measure how long it takes to execute once.

  2. Policy  
     • Threshold: **1000 ms** (1 second).  
     • If the measured duration is ≤ 1000 ms ⇒ VERDICT = PASS, otherwise
       VERDICT = FAIL.

  3. Where to store the evidence  
     • Directory: **/home/user/benchmark_logs**  
       (create it if it does not exist).  
     • File   : **/home/user/benchmark_logs/performance_policy.log**

  4. Exact log-file format (7 plain-text lines, *no* extra spaces)  
     Line-1: POLICY_NAME: quick_true_execution  
     Line-2: COMMAND: /bin/true  
     Line-3: START_EPOCH_MS: <13-digit integer>  
     Line-4: END_EPOCH_MS: <13-digit integer>  
     Line-5: DURATION_MS: <integer>  
     Line-6: THRESHOLD_MS: 1000  
     Line-7: VERDICT: PASS|FAIL            ← exactly one of these words

     • “<13-digit integer>” means numeric epoch time in milliseconds
       (e.g. 1695148823123).  
     • The field names and colons must appear exactly as written; the
       automated grader performs a strict line-by-line comparison of the
       labels and will only tolerate differing numeric values on the
       START/END/DURATION lines and the word PASS/FAIL on line 7.

  5. Hints (do *not* list these commands directly in your answer)  
     • You can capture high-resolution epoch time with “date +%s%3N” or
       with nanoseconds and then divide.  
     • Store each measured value in a shell variable, perform the
       arithmetic, then echo the seven lines into the log file.  
     • Make sure the final verdict respects the threshold rule.

  Expected end state: the file
  /home/user/benchmark_logs/performance_policy.log exists and matches
  the exact 7-line format above; line 6 must read “THRESHOLD_MS: 1000”,
  and line 7 must say either “VERDICT: PASS” (very likely) or
  “VERDICT: FAIL” if the measurement exceeds the threshold.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth used by automated tests; do not reveal in the public
       task description.                                    -->
  <expected_log_file>/home/user/benchmark_logs/performance_policy.log</expected_log_file>

  <!-- Regular expressions to validate each line. -->
  <line1_regex>^POLICY_NAME: quick_true_execution$</line1_regex>
  <line2_regex>^COMMAND: /bin/true$</line2_regex>
  <line3_regex>^START_EPOCH_MS: [0-9]{13}$</line3_regex>
  <line4_regex>^END_EPOCH_MS: [0-9]{13}$</line4_regex>
  <line5_regex>^DURATION_MS: [0-9]+$</line5_regex>
  <line6_regex>^THRESHOLD_MS: 1000$</line6_regex>
  <line7_regex>^VERDICT: (PASS|FAIL)$</line7_regex>

  <!-- Additional test: if VERDICT is PASS, DURATION_MS must be ≤1000;
       if VERDICT is FAIL, it must be >1000.    -->
name: task_000000_ea836d25
